description: End-to-end diagnosis pipeline evaluation. 10 synthetic scenarios testing cross-ingredient reasoning, each with a meal timeline, pre-computed ingredient stats, and ground truth triggers/bystanders.

scenarios.name: Single trigger with co-occurring bystander
scenarios.description: Wheat is the sole trigger. Cheddar cheese frequently co-occurs but is innocent. Key test: 3 cheese-only meals with no symptoms prove cheese is a bystander.

ingredients_to_analyze.medical_context: Wheat contains fructans (FODMAPs) and gluten. Fructans are rapidly fermented by gut bacteria causing bloating and gas. Wheat is one of the most common IBS triggers and is the first food eliminated on a low-FODMAP diet. Gluten may also cause symptoms in non-coeliac gluten sensitivity. Source: Monash University FODMAP App (red-light); Biesiekierski et al. 2011.

Hard cheeses like cheddar are low-FODMAP. The aging process breaks down most lactose. Cheddar contains less than 0.1g lactose per serve compared to 12g in milk. Safe on low-FODMAP diets. Source: Monash University FODMAP App (green-light); British Dietetic Association.

Butter is almost pure fat with minimal lactose (0.1g per serve). It is low-FODMAP and well-tolerated by most lactose-intolerant individuals. Used freely on elimination diets. Source: Monash University FODMAP App (green-light).

scenarios.meal_history_summary: 20 meals over 3 weeks. Wheat appeared in 8 meals with symptoms following all 8. Cheddar cheese appeared in 9 meals, 5 with symptoms — but all 5 symptomatic cheese meals also contained wheat. Three cheese-only meals (no wheat) produced zero symptoms. Butter appeared in 7 meals, always alongside wheat or cheese.

key_evidence: Wheat present in all 8 symptomatic meals (100%)
3 cheese-only meals with no symptoms prove cheese is innocent
2 wheat-only meals (no cheese) both caused symptoms
Butter always co-occurs with wheat — confounded, not independent

scenarios.name: Two independent triggers with different temporal signatures
scenarios.description: Garlic causes immediate symptoms (1-2hr). Cream causes delayed symptoms (6-12hr). Chicken and rice are innocent bystanders confounded by both triggers.

meals.name: Plain chicken and rice
meals.note: No garlic, no cream — NO symptoms

ingredients_to_analyze.medical_context: Garlic is one of the highest-FODMAP foods, rich in fructans. Fructans are not absorbed in the small intestine and are rapidly fermented by gut bacteria causing bloating, gas, and cramping. Garlic is the most commonly identified trigger in IBS patients. Source: Monash University FODMAP App (red-light at any quantity); Staudacher et al. 2012.

Cream is high in lactose and saturated fat. A significant trigger for lactose-intolerant individuals and those with IBS. Lactose intolerance affects 65-70% of global adult population. High fat content slows gastric emptying. Source: Monash University FODMAP App; Suchy et al. 2010.

Chicken breast is a lean, low-fat protein with no fibre, no FODMAPs, and no known allergens (outside rare poultry allergy). Universally recommended on elimination diets including low-FODMAP, GAPS, and autoimmune protocol diets. Source: Monash University FODMAP App; NICE IBS guidelines.

White rice is the most easily digestible starch. It is low-FODMAP, gluten-free, and contains no known digestive irritants. Standard 'safe food' on elimination diets worldwide. Source: Monash University FODMAP App; American College of Gastroenterology.

scenarios.meal_history_summary: 16 meals over 2 weeks. Garlic appeared in 7 meals — all 7 followed by immediate symptoms (1-2hr). Cream appeared in 5 meals — all 5 followed by delayed symptoms (6-12hr). Chicken appeared in 8 meals but 3 without garlic/cream had no symptoms. Rice appeared in 10 meals but 2 without triggers had no symptoms. Two distinct temporal signatures.

key_evidence: Garlic → 100% immediate symptoms (1-2hr lag), consistent pattern
Cream → 100% delayed symptoms (6-12hr lag), different temporal signature
4 chicken-only or rice-only meals with no symptoms
Two independent triggers with distinct onset patterns

scenarios.name: High co-occurrence confounder
scenarios.description: Onion is the real trigger. Garlic appears in 90% of onion meals, creating a strong confounding signal. Key test: 2 garlic-only meals with no symptoms prove garlic is innocent here.

meals.name: Grilled chicken
meals.note: No onion, no garlic — NO symptoms

meals.name: Salmon with rice
meals.note: No onion, no garlic — NO symptoms

meals.name: Garlic mushroom pasta
meals.note: Garlic-only — NO symptoms (mushroom may be a trigger in other contexts but garlic-only point stands)

meals.name: Caramelised onion tart
meals.note: Onion WITHOUT garlic — symptoms follow, proving onion is independent trigger

ingredients_to_analyze.medical_context: Onion is high-FODMAP, containing significant fructans and fructose. Along with garlic, onion is one of the two most commonly identified triggers in IBS and FODMAP-sensitive individuals. Source: Monash University FODMAP App (red-light); NICE IBS guidelines.

Garlic is one of the highest-FODMAP foods, rich in fructans. However, in THIS scenario garlic appears in 12 meals but only 7 had symptoms — and all 7 also contained onion. 3 garlic-only meals (no onion) produced zero symptoms. The garlic signal is confounded by onion co-occurrence.

Olive oil is a monounsaturated fat with no FODMAPs, no allergens, and no digestive irritants. Used as the recommended cooking fat on low-FODMAP and elimination diets. Source: Monash University FODMAP guidelines.

scenarios.meal_history_summary: 15 meals over 2 weeks. Onion in 8 meals — all 8 caused symptoms. Garlic in 12 meals — 7 caused symptoms, but all 7 also had onion. Crucially, 3 garlic-only meals (no onion) had zero symptoms. Olive oil in 11 meals — confounded by both onion and garlic.

key_evidence: Onion present in all 8 symptomatic meals (100%)
3 garlic-only meals (no onion) had zero symptoms
1 onion-only meal (no garlic, meal 13) still caused symptoms
Garlic co-occurs with onion in 88% of onion meals — confounded

scenarios.name: State-dependent trigger
scenarios.description: Raw onion causes symptoms; cooked onion does not. Tests whether the system can distinguish ingredient state as a factor.

ingredients_to_analyze.medical_context: Raw onion contains concentrated fructans and fructose, which are FODMAPs. Raw onion retains more fructans than cooked — cooking breaks down some fructans through heat. Raw onion is one of the most potent IBS triggers. Source: Monash University FODMAP App (red-light raw); Muir et al. 2009.

Cooked onion still contains some fructans but at reduced levels compared to raw. Some individuals tolerate cooked onion but not raw. In this user's history, 5 cooked onion meals produced zero symptoms. Source: Monash University FODMAP App.

Tomatoes are low-FODMAP in standard portions. They are acidic and can exacerbate GERD but do not typically cause bloating or cramping. In this user's data, 1 tomato-only meal (no onion) produced no symptoms. Source: Monash University FODMAP App.

scenarios.meal_history_summary: 14 meals over 2 weeks. Raw onion in 5 meals — all 5 caused symptoms. Cooked onion in 5 meals — zero symptoms. Tomato in 7 meals — 4 symptoms, but all 4 also had raw onion. 1 tomato-only meal had no symptoms.

key_evidence: 5/5 raw onion meals caused symptoms (100%)
0/5 cooked onion meals caused symptoms (0%)
Same ingredient, different state = different outcome
1 tomato-only meal (no raw onion) had no symptoms

scenarios.name: Small sample with strong allergic signal
scenarios.description: Only 3 peanut meals but all caused severe symptoms within 30 minutes. Classic allergic pattern with small sample but unmistakable signal.

meals.name: Cashew stir-fry
meals.note: Cashew but no peanut — NO symptoms

meals.name: Cashew chicken
meals.note: Cashew but no peanut — NO symptoms

meals.name: Cashew trail mix snack
meals.note: Cashew but no peanut — NO symptoms

meals.name: Cashew curry
meals.note: Cashew but no peanut — NO symptoms

ingredients_to_analyze.medical_context: Peanut is a top-9 FDA allergen. Peanut allergy is one of the most common food allergies, often causing severe GI symptoms including cramping, nausea, and vomiting within minutes. The rapid onset (under 30 minutes) and high severity are characteristic of IgE-mediated allergy. Source: FDA Major Allergens; Sicherer & Sampson 2014.

Cashews are tree nuts (separate allergen category from peanuts, which are legumes). While tree nut allergy exists, cashew and peanut allergies are independent. In this user's data, 4 cashew meals produced zero symptoms. Source: FDA Major Allergens.

scenarios.meal_history_summary: 12 meals over 2 weeks. Peanut appeared in only 3 meals — all 3 caused severe symptoms within 30 minutes (severity 7-9). Cashew appeared in 4 meals with zero symptoms. The rapid onset and high severity suggest an allergic mechanism rather than FODMAP intolerance.

key_evidence: 3/3 peanut meals caused severe symptoms (100%)
Extremely rapid onset (20-30 min) and high severity (8-9) suggest allergy
0/4 cashew meals caused symptoms — different nut category, independent
Small sample but unmistakable signal: 100% hit rate, severe, immediate

scenarios.name: Red herring — no real trigger
scenarios.description: No ingredient is a real trigger. Random symptom timing creates spurious correlations. Potato correlates 3/4 by chance. Tests whether the system avoids false confidence.

symptoms.note: Mild, could be anything — stress, eating speed, etc.
Mild — no potato in this meal
Potato present but mild, same as other meals

ingredients_to_analyze.medical_context: Potatoes are low-FODMAP, gluten-free, and contain no known digestive irritants. One of the safest starches for sensitive digestive systems. The 67% symptom rate (4/6) looks suspicious but all symptoms were mild (severity 3-4) and could be coincidental. Source: Monash University FODMAP App.

Carrots are low-FODMAP at standard portions and contain no known digestive irritants. One of the safest vegetables for sensitive digestive systems. Source: Monash University FODMAP App; British Dietetic Association IBS guidelines.
White rice is the most easily digestible starch. Low-FODMAP, gluten-free, no known digestive irritants. Standard 'safe food' used in elimination diets worldwide. Source: Monash University FODMAP App.

scenarios.meal_history_summary: 14 meals over 2 weeks. 5 mild symptom episodes scattered randomly. Potato present in 4/5 symptomatic meals (67%) but absent from 1 symptomatic meal. All symptoms are mild severity (3-4). No ingredient shows a convincing pattern — symptoms appear random and likely caused by non-dietary factors (stress, eating speed, etc.).

key_evidence: No ingredient has 100% correlation — potato misses 1 symptomatic meal
All symptoms are mild (severity 3-4) — not characteristic of food intolerance
Potato, carrot, and rice are all medically safe (low-FODMAP, no allergens)
Symptom pattern is random — likely non-dietary causes

scenarios.name: Multiple triggers, one masked by the other
scenarios.description: Both wheat and dairy are triggers, but dairy's signal is partially masked because 3 of its symptomatic meals also contained wheat. Key test: 2 dairy-only meals still caused symptoms.

meals.name: Plain chicken and rice
meals.note: No wheat, no dairy — NO symptoms

meals.name: Grilled chicken salad
meals.note: No wheat, no dairy — NO symptoms

meals.name: Rice with steamed veg
meals.note: No wheat, no dairy — NO symptoms

meals.name: Cereal with milk
meals.note: Dairy-only (no wheat) — SYMPTOMS (proving dairy is independent trigger)

meals.name: Chicken lettuce wrap
meals.note: No wheat, no dairy — NO symptoms

meals.name: Salmon with rice
meals.note: No wheat, no dairy — NO symptoms

symptoms.note: Dairy-only — symptoms confirm dairy is trigger

ingredients_to_analyze.medical_context: Wheat contains fructans (FODMAPs) and gluten. Fructans are rapidly fermented by gut bacteria causing bloating and gas. One of the most common IBS triggers. Source: Monash University FODMAP App (red-light); Biesiekierski et al. 2011.

Dairy products (milk, cream, soft cheese, yoghurt) contain lactose. Lactose intolerance affects 65-70% of global adult population. Symptoms include bloating, cramping, gas, and diarrhoea. Source: NIH Genetics Home Reference; Monash University FODMAP App.
Chicken breast is a lean, low-fat protein with no FODMAPs and no known allergens. Universally safe on elimination diets. Source: Monash University FODMAP App.
Lettuce is low-FODMAP, extremely low in fermentable carbohydrates. Mostly water and fibre. No known allergens or intolerance triggers. Source: Monash University FODMAP App.

scenarios.meal_history_summary: 16 meals over 2+ weeks. Wheat in 10 meals — 9 caused symptoms (90%). Dairy in 6 meals — 5 caused symptoms (83%), but 3 of those also had wheat. Crucially: 2 dairy-only meals (no wheat) both caused symptoms, confirming dairy is an independent trigger. 5 meals with neither wheat nor dairy — zero symptoms.

key_evidence: Wheat present in 9/11 symptomatic meals, 90% hit rate
2 dairy-only meals (meals 8, 14) both caused symptoms — dairy is independent trigger
3 dairy+wheat meals had worse symptoms (severity 7-8) than either alone (5-6)
5 meals with neither trigger had zero symptoms

scenarios.name: Cumulative dose effect
scenarios.description: Single coffee is usually fine. 3+ coffees in a day triggers symptoms. Tests whether the system can recognise dose-dependent patterns.

ingredients_to_analyze.medical_context: Coffee stimulates gastric acid secretion and accelerates colonic motility. Caffeine is a known GI stimulant and has a dose-dependent effect — cumulative intake within a day compounds the motility stimulation. A well-documented trigger for IBS, especially at higher daily doses. Source: Boekema et al. 1999; Rao et al. 1998.

Milk contains lactose but the small amounts in coffee (splash of milk) are typically below the threshold for lactose intolerance symptoms. In this user's data, many single-coffee-with-milk meals had no symptoms. Source: Monash University FODMAP App.

Table sugar (sucrose) is well-absorbed and is not a FODMAP. It contains no allergens or known digestive irritants at normal quantities. Not a recognised IBS trigger. Source: Monash University FODMAP App.

scenarios.meal_history_summary: 18 entries over 9 days. Coffee appeared 14 times. Single-coffee days (5 days) had zero symptoms. On 3 days with 3+ coffees, symptoms occurred every time. Pattern: dose-dependent. Milk appeared in 9 coffee entries, sugar in 6 — both present in both symptomatic and asymptomatic coffees, ruling them out as triggers.

key_evidence: 3/3 days with 3+ coffees caused symptoms (100%)
0/5 single-coffee days caused symptoms (0%)
Dose-dependent pattern: more coffees = symptoms, single coffee = fine
Milk and sugar present in both symptomatic and asymptomatic entries — confounded

scenarios.name: Known FODMAP with textbook-clear stats
scenarios.description: Garlic is in every symptomatic meal and absent from all asymptomatic meals. Textbook case that the system should ace.

ingredients_to_analyze.medical_context: Garlic is one of the highest-FODMAP foods, rich in fructans. Fructans are not absorbed in the small intestine and are rapidly fermented by gut bacteria causing bloating, gas, and cramping. The most commonly identified trigger in IBS patients. Source: Monash University FODMAP App (red-light at any quantity); Staudacher et al. 2012.

Wheat pasta in standard portions (1 cup cooked) is low-FODMAP according to Monash testing. While wheat contains fructans, the amount in a normal pasta serving is below the trigger threshold for most people. Source: Monash University FODMAP App; Gibson & Shepherd 2010.
Chicken breast is a lean, low-fat protein with no FODMAPs and no known allergens. Universally safe on elimination diets. Source: Monash University FODMAP App.
Olive oil is a monounsaturated fat with no FODMAPs, no allergens, and no digestive irritants. Recommended cooking fat on low-FODMAP and elimination diets. Source: Monash University FODMAP guidelines.

scenarios.meal_history_summary: 15 meals over 2 weeks. Perfect split: 8 meals with garlic — all 8 caused symptoms. 7 meals without garlic — zero symptoms. Olive oil appeared in 10 meals (7 with garlic, 3 without) — the 3 without garlic had no symptoms. Pasta in 5 meals (4 with garlic) — the pattern follows garlic, not pasta.

key_evidence: Garlic in 8/8 symptomatic meals (100%), absent from 7/7 asymptomatic meals (100%)
Perfect binary split — garlic is necessary and sufficient for symptoms
Olive oil in 3 asymptomatic meals (without garlic) — proves oil is innocent
Pasta in 1 asymptomatic meal or only with garlic — confounded but pasta is low-FODMAP

scenarios.name: Mixed evidence with borderline trigger
scenarios.description: Wheat correlates strongly but 2 wheat-only meals had no symptoms. Tests whether the system identifies wheat with appropriate uncertainty rather than absolute confidence.

meals.name: Plain toast
meals.note: Wheat-only meal — NO symptoms (second contradicting meal)

ingredients_to_analyze.medical_context: Wheat contains fructans (FODMAPs) and gluten. Fructans are rapidly fermented by gut bacteria causing bloating and gas. However, individual sensitivity varies — some people tolerate small portions (e.g., single slice of bread) but react to larger amounts. The 80% symptom rate (8/10) with 2 asymptomatic wheat meals is consistent with dose-dependent sensitivity. Source: Monash University FODMAP App; Biesiekierski et al. 2011.
Hard cheeses like cheddar are low-FODMAP. The aging process breaks down most lactose. Safe on low-FODMAP diets. Source: Monash University FODMAP App (green-light).

Eggs are a top-9 FDA allergen but also a very common food. Egg intolerance can cause GI symptoms in some individuals. In this user's data, eggs appeared 4 times — 2 with symptoms (both also had wheat), 2 without symptoms (1 had wheat). Insufficient evidence. Source: FDA Major Allergens.

scenarios.meal_history_summary: 15 meals over 2 weeks. Wheat appeared in 10 meals — 8 caused symptoms (80%), but 2 wheat-only meals had NO symptoms (meals 7, 14). This contradicts a simple 'wheat always causes symptoms' model. Cheese in 5 meals — 3 symptoms, but all 3 also had wheat. Egg in 4 meals — confounded by wheat. 5 wheat-free meals — zero symptoms.

key_evidence: 8/10 wheat meals caused symptoms (80%) — strong but not perfect correlation
2 wheat-only meals had no symptoms — possibly dose-dependent or variable sensitivity
0/5 wheat-free meals caused symptoms — wheat is necessary condition
Wheat should be identified but with qualified confidence, not certainty
